summary: A West African empire (c. 1235-1670) founded by Sundiata Keita after his defeat of the Sosso king Sumanguru Kante at the Battle of Kirina, and later ruled by the fabulously wealthy Mansa Musa, whose gold-laden hajj to Mecca devalued gold prices in Egypt. It shares its name with the modern country of Mali (capital Bamako), governed itself through the Gbara assembly under a 44-article constitution called the Kouroukan Fouga, and grew rich as a hub of the trans-Saharan salt-and-gold trade centered on Timbuktu and its Sankore mosque-university. Its founding is recounted in the oral Epic of Sundiata, preserved by griots, and it eventually declined after military defeats, giving rise to the successor Songhai Empire.

works.name: General / Giveaway Identifiers
works.description: The empire is most commonly identified as having been founded by Sundiata Keita and later ruled by Mansa Musa, and as sharing its name with the modern-day West African country of Mali, whose capital is Bamako. Its founding is also frequently identified through its recounting in the oral Epic of Sundiata, a history preserved and performed by griots (hereditary poet-historians).

clues.clue: Founded by Sundiata Keita and later ruled by Mansa Musa
examples: name this empire founded by Sundiata Keita and later ruled by Mansa Musa.
name this West African empire led by Sundiata and Mansa Musa.

clues.clue: Shares its name with the modern-day country of Mali, whose capital is Bamako
examples: name this empire whose leaders included Sundiata Keita and Mansa Musa, which shares its name with a modern-day country whose capital is Bamako.
Sundiata Keita founded this dynasty in Western Africa. It shares its name with a modern country with a capital at Bamako.

clues.clue: The empire's founding is recounted in the oral Epic of Sundiata, preserved and performed by griots
examples: name this empire, whose formation is recounted in the oral Epic of Sundiata.
Talking drums often accompany "praise singers" called griots, who orally preserved many poems from this West African empire, including the Epic of Sundiata.

works.name: Founding: Sundiata Keita & the Battle of Kirina
works.description: The empire's founder, Sundiata Keita, was legendarily disabled and did not walk until age seven, fulfilling a prophecy that he would marry the hunchbacked Sogolon. He united the region's Mandinka towns by defeating the Sosso king Sumanguru Kante at the Battle of Kirina; one retelling has Sundiata's forces wounding Sumanguru with an arrow tipped with a white rooster's (or cock's) spur after a taunting exchange of boasts between the two rivals. Following this victory, the towns that had allied against Sosso became known as the empire's "Twelve Doors," and the empire went on to conquer the older Empire of Ghana in 1235.

clues.clue: Founder Sundiata Keita was legendarily disabled, not walking until age seven, fulfilling a prophecy that he would marry the hunchbacked Sogolon
examples: This empire's first ruler did not walk until he was seven years old, according to a history that was recounted orally by griots.
In an epic poem from this country, a king fulfils a prophecy by marrying a hunchbacked woman named Sogolon.

clues.clue: Defeated the Sosso king Sumanguru Kante at the Battle of Kirina to found the empire
examples: This empire's legendarily disabled founder defeated Sumanguru Kante at the Battle of Kirina.
Its founder won a battle at Kirina over the Soso leader Sumanguru, and a later leader of it lowered the value of Egypt's gold when he went on the hajj.

clues.clue: In one retelling, after a taunting exchange of boasts ("the poisonous mushroom" vs. "the ravenous cock"), Sumanguru was wounded by an arrow tipped with a white rooster's spur
examples: In an argument over the rulership of this country, one character describes himself as "the poisonous mushroom that makes the fearless vomit," to which his enemy responds "I am the ravenous cock, the poison does not matter to me."
That character, the son of Maghann the Handsome, defeats an invader of this country by shooting him with an arrow tipped with a white rooster's spur.

clues.clue: After Kirina, the allied towns became known as the empire's "Twelve Doors," and the empire conquered the older Empire of Ghana in 1235
examples: The towns that united before the founding of this empire came to be known as its "Twelve Doors."
The ancient Empire of Ghana, for which the modern country was named, was conquered in 1235 by this African empire based in Timbuktu.

works.name: Government: The Gbara Assembly & the Kouroukan Fouga
works.description: After Kirina, the empire adopted a 44-article constitution known as the Kouroukan Fouga, covering social organization, property, environmental preservation, and funerary customs. Governance ran through the Gbara, a 32-member deliberative "Great Assembly" whose first meeting brought together 29 clans presided over by a belen-tigui (master of ceremony). Society was organized into distinct clans: four princely maghan clans, sixteen "quiver-carrier" clans responsible for military defense, and seven nyamakala clans believed to possess a special creative force enabling their crafts and art forms; territorial governors were known as farbas.

clues.clue: The Kouroukan Fouga, a 44-article constitution covering social organization, goods, environmental preservation, and funerary customs, was adopted after the Battle of Kirina
examples: A set of 44 articles sometimes identified as this empire's constitution is divided into sections on social organization, goods, preserving nature, and final disposals.
Its constitution, the Kouroukan Fouga, was created after a battle in which the founder of this empire defeated a king of the Sosso.

clues.clue: Governance ran through the Gbara, a 32-member "Great Assembly" whose first meeting brought 29 clans together under a belen-tigui
examples: This empire's deliberative body was the "Great Assembly," whose first meeting had 29 clans presided over by a belen-tigui, or master of ceremony.
It also had sixteen "quiver-carrier" clans responsible for defense, who made up half of the 32-member legislative body known as the Gbara.

clues.clue: Clans were organized into four princely maghan clans, sixteen "quiver-carrier" clans for defense, and seven creative nyamakala clans for arts and crafts
examples: This empire distinguished between military "carriers of quivers," princely clans, and "wielders of energy."
Seven clans in this empire were said to possess a creative force which allowed them to make various products and art forms, and were known as the nyamakala clans.

clues.clue: Territorial governors were called farbas
examples: Its territorial governors were called farbas, and Mansa Musa was one ruler of this empire, the namesake of a modern day country.

works.name: Mansa Musa & the Pilgrimage to Mecca
works.description: The empire's most famous ruler, Mansa Musa, is best remembered for a lavishly wealthy hajj to Mecca, reportedly bringing as many as 60,000 men and enough gold that his spending devalued the price of gold across Egypt for years afterward. He commissioned the Djinguereber Mosque and the mudbrick learning center at Sankore in Timbuktu, works traditionally credited to the Granadan Sufi poet-architect al-Sahili. A famous depiction of him appears in the Catalan Atlas, shown holding a fleur-de-lis staff in one hand and a gold nugget in the other.

clues.clue: Devalued the price of gold in Egypt during his lavish hajj to Mecca
examples: This empire was founded by Sundiata, and a later ruler of this empire devalued gold all along his route during his hajj to Mecca.
One king of this empire built the Djinguereber Mosque and the University of Sankore, but is more famous for single handedly devaluing gold in the region because of his lavish pilgrimage to Mecca.

clues.clue: Commissioned the Djinguereber Mosque and the mudbrick learning center at Sankore, traditionally credited to the Granadan poet-architect al-Sahili
examples: Architectural works built under this empire like the Djinguereber Mosque have traditionally been credited to the Sufi poet al-Sahili.
This empire's landmarks included a learning center at Sankore and a religious building at Djenne, both made of mudbrick.

clues.clue: Depicted in the Catalan Atlas holding a fleur-de-lis staff in one hand and a gold nugget in the other
examples: One map of this empire in the Catalan Atlas shows its leader holding a fleur-de-lis staff in one hand and looking to the left at a gold nugget he holds in the other.

clues.clue: Reportedly brought as many as 60,000 men with him on the hajj
examples: This West African Empire was founded by Sundiata Keita and ruled by the infamously rich Mansa Musa, who reportedly took 60,000 men with him on hajj.

works.name: Later Mansas & Succession Crises
works.description: Later rulers of the empire included Sakura, a formerly enslaved freedman who usurped the throne from the founder's grandson as the empire's fifth ruler but reportedly saved it from a political crisis and seized the copper-producing Takedda region. Other mansas are remembered through anecdote: one gave a European traveler only bread, fried meat, and sour milk and made his court poets wear bird masks; the Chronicle of the Seeker follows a mansa who accidentally killed his own mother; and a mansa is said to have vanished into a "great whirlpool" at sea on an exploratory voyage before the husband of Inare Kunati took the throne.

clues.clue: Sakura, a formerly enslaved freedman, usurped the throne as the empire's fifth ruler from the founder's grandson, reportedly saving the empire from crisis, and seized the copper-producing Takedda region
examples: The fifth leader of this empire was a freedman who usurped the throne from the grandson of this empire's founder.
It's not Japan, but this empire's leaders included Sakoura, who seized the copper-producing Takedda region.

clues.clue: One mansa gave a traveler only bread, fried meat, and sour milk, and made his court poets wear bird masks
examples: One ruler of this type only gave a traveler bread, fried meat, and sour milk, and made his court poets wear bird masks.

clues.clue: The Chronicle of the Seeker follows a mansa who accidentally killed his own mother
examples: The Chronicle of the Seeker follows another of these rulers who killed his mother by accident.

clues.clue: A mansa is said to have vanished into a "great whirlpool" at sea on an exploratory voyage before the husband of Inare Kunati took the throne
examples: One of these men vanished in a "great whirlpool" at sea before the husband of Inare Kunati took this title.

works.name: Society & Economy
works.description: The traveler Ibn Battuta, who visited the empire, praised the diligence of local children in memorizing the Qur'an but complained that slaves and women went about topless; he also observed the mansas' chief interpreter, the jeli, a precursor to the later griots. By the empire's later years it was ruled primarily by the Bambara and Mandinka ethnic groups, and it prospered throughout its history as a hub of the trans-Saharan salt-and-gold trade.

clues.clue: A foreign traveler (Ibn Battuta) praised local children for their diligence in learning the Qur'an but complained that slaves and women here went about topless
examples: A foreign traveler praised children in this state for their diligence in learning the Qur'an but complained that slaves and women here freely went around topless.

clues.clue: Ibn Battuta observed the mansas' chief interpreter, the jeli, a precursor to the later griots
examples: Ibn Battuta saw these leaders' chief interpreter, or jeli, a precursor of later oral poets called griots.

clues.clue: By its later years, was ruled primarily by the Bambara and Mandinka ethnic groups
examples: This empire, which lost the Battle of Djenne to Morocco, was ruled primarily by the Bambara and Mandinka ethnic groups.

clues.clue: Prospered as a hub of the trans-Saharan salt-and-gold trade
examples: Name this salt and gold trading empire founded by Sundiata Keita.

works.name: Cities & Landmarks
works.description: The empire's early capital was Niani, but its greatest cultural hub was Timbuktu, home to the Sankore mosque-university and reached by goods passing through the trading city of Djenne, itself known for its mudbrick Great Mosque.

clues.clue: Niani was an early capital of the empire
examples: Niani was an early capital for these people.

clues.clue: Timbuktu was the empire's major cultural hub, and its cities also included Djenne
examples: Timbuktu was this empire's major cultural hub.
The Songhai Empire was established after a revolt against this other West African empire once led by Mansa Musa whose cities included Timbuktu.

works.name: Decline & Aftermath
works.description: The empire eventually lost the Battle of Djenne to Morocco. Under its final ruler, Mahmud IV, the Tarikh al-Sudan records that its troops, split into two divisions led by the sanqara-zuma and farim-soura, were unable to save him in the empire's final battle. The Songhai Empire, whose cities came to include Timbuktu, was established following a revolt against Mali.

clues.clue: Lost the Battle of Djenne to Morocco
examples: This empire, which lost the Battle of Djenne to Morocco, was ruled primarily by the Bambara and Mandinka ethnic groups.

clues.clue: Under final ruler Mahmud IV, the Tarikh al-Sudan records that its troops, split between the sanqara-zuma and farim-soura divisions, could not save him in the empire's final battle
examples: The Tarikh al-Sudan records that this state's troops were split into two divisions, led by the sanqara-zuma and farim-soura, who were unable to aid its ruler Mahmud IV in its final battle.

clues.clue: The Songhai Empire was established following a revolt against Mali
examples: The Songhai Empire was established after a revolt against this other West African empire once led by Mansa Musa whose cities included Timbuktu.

works.name: Legacy: The Epic of Sundiata & Modern Depictions
works.description: The empire's founding survives chiefly through the Epic of Sundiata, an oral history preserved by griots in which the griot Balla Fasseke sings the "Hymn to the Bow" after Sundiata bends an iron bar while learning to stand, and an army sings "take your bow and let us go"; the epic was translated into English by D.T. Niane. The modern Malian writer Amadou Hampate Ba studied the same oral tradition, including the fictional griot Balla Fasseke, while the novel Bound to Violence — following a character named Raymond Spartacus Kassoumi who returns to the fictional nation of Nakem-Zuiko as it seeks independence — was accused of plagiarizing Graham Greene.

clues.clue: In the Epic of Sundiata, the griot Balla Fasseke sings the "Hymn to the Bow" after Sundiata bends an iron bar while learning to stand; an army sings "take your bow and let us go"
examples: In that poem from this country, a jali named Balla composes the "song of the bow" to honour a man who bent a mighty iron rod into a curve.
In that work from what became this country, an army sings, "Take your bow and let us go" in the "Hymn to the Bow," as translated by D.T. Niane.

clues.clue: The modern writer Amadou Hampate Ba studied the same oral tradition, which included the fictional griot Balla Fasseke
examples: This country's author Amadou Hampate Ba studied an oral tradition that included the fictional Balla Fasseke.

clues.clue: The novel Bound to Violence follows Raymond Spartacus Kassoumi, who returns to the fictional nation of Nakem-Zuiko as it seeks independence, and was accused of plagiarizing Graham Greene
examples: In a novel from this country, Raymond Spartacus Kassoumi returns to the nation of Nakem-Zuiko, which attempts to seek independence.
That novel from this country, Bound to Violence, was accused of plagiarizing Graham Greene.

comprehensive_summary: The Mali Empire (c. 1235-1670) was a West African empire founded by Sundiata Keita, whose legendary disability (he did not walk until age seven) fulfilled a prophecy that he would marry the hunchbacked Sogolon. Sundiata united the region's Mandinka towns by defeating the Sosso king Sumanguru Kante at the Battle of Kirina — in one retelling, after a taunting exchange of boasts, Sumanguru was wounded by an arrow tipped with a white rooster's spur. The allied towns became known as the empire's "Twelve Doors," and the empire went on to conquer the older Empire of Ghana in 1235. Following Kirina, the empire adopted a 44-article constitution, the Kouroukan Fouga, covering social organization, property, environmental preservation, and funerary customs, and governed through the Gbara, a 32-member "Great Assembly" whose first meeting brought 29 clans together under a belen-tigui. Society was organized into four princely maghan clans, sixteen "quiver-carrier" clans responsible for defense, and seven creative nyamakala clans believed to possess a special creative force for arts and crafts, while territorial governors were called farbas. The empire's most famous ruler, Mansa Musa, is remembered above all for a lavishly wealthy hajj to Mecca — reportedly with as many as 60,000 men — during which his spending devalued the price of gold across Egypt; he commissioned the Djinguereber Mosque and the mudbrick learning center at Sankore in Timbuktu, works traditionally credited to the Granadan poet-architect al-Sahili, and is depicted in the Catalan Atlas holding a fleur-de-lis staff and a gold nugget. Later rulers included Sakura, a formerly enslaved freedman who usurped the throne from the founder's grandson as the fifth mansa but reportedly saved the empire from crisis and seized the copper-producing Takedda region; other mansas are remembered through anecdotes, including one who served a traveler only bread, fried meat, and sour milk and made his court poets wear bird masks, one whose accidental killing of his own mother is recorded in the Chronicle of the Seeker, and one said to have vanished into a "great whirlpool" at sea on an exploratory voyage before the husband of Inare Kunati took the throne. The traveler Ibn Battuta, who visited the empire, praised local children's diligence in memorizing the Qur'an but complained that slaves and women went about topless, and observed the mansas' chief interpreter, the jeli, a precursor to the later griots. The empire's early capital was Niani, but Timbuktu became its major cultural hub, reached via the trading city of Djenne, and the empire prospered as a hub of the trans-Saharan salt-and-gold trade. By its later years it was ruled primarily by the Bambara and Mandinka ethnic groups; it lost the Battle of Djenne to Morocco, and under its final ruler, Mahmud IV, the Tarikh al-Sudan records that its troops — split between the sanqara-zuma and farim-soura divisions — could not save him in the empire's final battle. The Songhai Empire, whose cities came to include Timbuktu, was established following a revolt against Mali. The empire's founding survives chiefly through the oral Epic of Sundiata, preserved by griots, in which the griot Balla Fasseke sings the "Hymn to the Bow" after Sundiata bends an iron bar while learning to stand, and an army sings "take your bow and let us go"; the epic was translated into English by D.T. Niane. The modern Malian writer Amadou Hampate Ba studied the same oral tradition, including the fictional griot Balla Fasseke, and the modern country of Mali, whose capital is Bamako, is named for the empire. The novel Bound to Violence, following a character named Raymond Spartacus Kassoumi who returns to the fictional nation of Nakem-Zuiko as it seeks independence, was accused of plagiarizing Graham Greene.
recursive_suggestions: Mansa Musa — extremely frequently clued individual ruler; could merit a standalone figure page if independent clue density grows beyond the empire's own page
Sundiata Keita — founder, currently clued only through the founding of Mali; own bonus part answerline seen (2013 VCU Closed)
mansas of the Mali Empire — appeared as its own bonus answerline (2015 ACF Regionals) collectively covering rulers; folded into the 'Later Mansas' section here
Songhai Empire — successor state after a revolt against Mali; already queued/exists as its own topic
Empire of Ghana (Wagadu Empire) — earlier West African empire conquered by Mali in 1235; appeared as its own bonus answerline (2017 Oxford Open), excluded here
Mutapa Empire — unrelated Southern African empire that appeared paired with Mali in a bonus (2017 Oxford Open); excluded
Battle of Kirina — recurring event with its own bonus answerline (2021 COOT); folded into the founding narrative here
Djenne — recurring city with its own bonus answerlines (2025 NASAT, 2015 Oxford Open); excluded those specific parts
University of Sankore — recurring institution with its own bonus answerlines (2020 ACF Winter, 2015 Oxford Open); excluded those specific parts
Griots — recurring cultural role with its own bonus answerlines (2023 ILLIAC, 2012 Michigan KABO); excluded those specific parts
Mandinka — recurring ethnic group with its own bonus answerline (2014 Penn Bowl); excluded that specific part

Excluded roughly two dozen bonus parts whose actual answers were Twelve Doors of Mali, Djenne, griots, a bow, Bamana Empire, Battle of Kirina, Negritude, Christopher Okigbo, University of Sankore, Morocco, Empire of Ghana, Mutapa Empire, Mandinka, Toure/Samori Toure, Sundiata Keita, Askia Mohammad I, Battle of Tondibi, Ghana (modern country), and Kwame Nkrumah
